trajectory_summary: This is a 90-second gatekeeper callback that achieves a surface-level reschedule but leaves the appointment structurally fragile on two counts: the format is confirmed as in-person (wrong per Ion's phone-appointment protocol) and the confirmation is made with a gatekeeper rather than Carol herself. Parker's legitimizing opener worked — the gatekeeper moved from screening to cooperating — but Parker then accepted the gatekeeper's terms entirely, including format and channel, without any protocol check. The call ends with a time on the calendar but no verbal handshake with the actual customer, no format correction, and no clarity on whether Carol is aware of or aligned with the appointment.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the outcome should be tagged 'tentative_reschedule_fragile' — a time was set (Friday 3PM) but with a gatekeeper, not Carol, in the wrong format (in-person vs. phone), with no button-up protocol executed. This appointment carries meaningful no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I believe we have it for an in person appointment. We should be available for an in person appointment at 3PM on Friday.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker confirmed in-person format twice with a gatekeeper, violating button_up.phone_appointment_specified, and executed zero button-up elements with the actual customer. This is the single highest-risk gap on the call — if Carol expects an in-person visit and the closer calls by phone (or vice versa), the appointment collapses at first contact. The manager should ask Parker directly: 'What format is this appointment, and how do you know?' Then walk through the correct reschedule protocol: confirm phone format, get Carol's direct number, plan a brief re-confirmation call with Carol before Friday.

key_moments.quote: We just had a a solar design built up for Carol that we weren't able to show her. I was just seeing if there's a better day to get her that information.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker's opener is the strongest move on the call — leading with 'a design already built' creates a low-friction reason for the gatekeeper to cooperate rather than screen. This is curiosity framing applied correctly at the gatekeeper layer. Strategic read holds: Parker is not trying to sell solar to the gatekeeper, just open the door to Carol.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. To kinda go over to the design or just to get a better time for you? Or
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker asks the gatekeeper whether the Friday call is to 'go over the design' or 'get a better time' — framing the appointment purpose as ambiguous and deferring to the gatekeeper to define it. This cedes frame ownership at a critical moment. The gatekeeper, not Parker, ends up defining the call's purpose ('you gotta go over the design'). Parker should have owned the frame: the Friday call is to show Carol the design Ion built for her home.

key_moments.quote: I believe we have it for an in person appointment. We should be available for an in person appointment at 3PM on Friday.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker confirms an in-person appointment format — twice — without any check against Ion's protocol that appointments are phone-based. This is a direct protocol violation. The 'I believe' hedge suggests Parker may not be certain of the format, which makes the double confirmation worse: Parker is locking in a format they're unsure about rather than clarifying.

key_moments.quote: Okay. Yeah. So, yeah, I had it for an appoint an in person at three on Friday.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is Parker's second confirmation of the in-person format — now with the gatekeeper after a brief interruption ('Go work. Go work.'). Parker had a natural reset opportunity when the gatekeeper stepped away and returned, but instead re-anchored the wrong format a second time. Two explicit in-person confirmations with the gatekeeper, zero corrections.
